svg.text: CSL — CHINESE SIGN LANGUAGE
HISTORY — DEAF EDUCATION IN EAST ASIA
CSL ORIGINS
1887: C.R. Mills opens first Deaf school in China (Shanghai)
American missionaries brought oral + sign methods; sign language survived
1949: PRC government standardizes education; CSL begins unification effort
1960s: Standard CSL dictionary issued; regional varieties persist
CSL IN THE PEOPLE'S REPUBLIC
China Disabled Persons' Federation (1988): oversees CSL standardization
CSL broadcasts on CCTV; sign interpreters in government events
~20M Deaf in China; vast majority use CSL
Shanghai vs Beijing dialectal signs: ongoing tension in standardization
CHINESE DEAF CULTURE
Deaf schools: historically boarding (same as most countries)
Strong Deaf community identity in major cities (Beijing, Shanghai)
KSL HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
KSL COLONIAL ORIGINS
1909: first Korean school for Deaf opens in Pyongyang (under Japanese rule)
Japanese teachers brought JSL (Japanese Sign Language) to Korea
KSL developed from JSL base; diverged significantly since 1945
NORTH AND SOUTH KSL
Korea divided 1945-1953: North and South Deaf communities separated
NKSL (North Korean): different from South KSL; diverged ~70+ years
South KSL: based in Seoul; officially recognized in Korea Welfare Law 2016
KSL RECOGNITION AND COMMUNITY
2016: KSL recognized as official language of Korea
~300,000 KSL users; Korean Deaf Association promotes KSL
KSL interpreters required for government events, courts, hospitals
CSL AND KSL: EAST ASIAN SIBLINGS
Both: emerged from Japanese/Western Deaf education in late 19th/early 20th c.
Both: SOV word order matching their spoken national languages
Both: officially recognized in 21st century after long advocacy
Both: large Deaf communities maintaining culture across political boundaries
PLATE 65d ALT · CSL / KSL · HISTORY
